title: Feldherrnhalle

infobox.value: ("Field Marshals' Hall") is a monumental [loggia](/wiki/Loggia "Loggia") on the [Odeonsplatz](/wiki/Odeonsplatz "Odeonsplatz") in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). Modelled after the [Loggia dei Lanzi](/wiki/Loggia_dei_Lanzi "Loggia dei Lanzi") in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), it was commissioned in 1841 by King [Ludwig I of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria") to honour the tradition of the [Bavarian Army](/wiki/Bavarian_Army "Bavarian Army").

In the town of Velden (Vils) in Bavaria the American [14th Armored Division](/wiki/14th_Armored_Division_(United_States) "14th Armored Division (United States)") found a [poison gas](/wiki/Poison_gas "Poison gas") factory.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Friedrich von Gärtner, [New International Encyclopedia](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_New_International_Encyclop%C3%A6dia/G%C3%A4rtner,_Friedrich_von "wikisource:The New International Encyclopædia/Gärtner, Friedrich von")
Joanna Egert-Romanowskiej et al, *DK Eyewitness Travel Guide: Germany: Germany*, p. 218.

Richard., Hanser (1971). [*Putsch! : How Hitler made revolution*](http://worldcat.org/oclc/2940685). Pyramid Publications. [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [2940685](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/2940685).

Limited, Alamy. ["Monument on the Munich Feldherrnhalle, Germany, 1936. Artist: Unknown Stock Photo"](https://www.alamy.com/monument-on-the-munich-feldherrnhalle-germany-1936-artist-unknown-image262767153.html). *www.alamy.com*. `

[![Listen to this article](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/20px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:En-Feldherrnhalle-article.ogg "Listen to this article")

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Monumental loggia in Munich, Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Feldherrnhalle_M%C3%BCnchen.jpg/330px-Feldherrnhalle_M%C3%BCnchen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Feldherrnhalle_M%C3%BCnchen.jpg)

The Feldherrnhalle on the [Odeonsplatz](/wiki/Odeonsplatz "Odeonsplatz")

The **Feldherrnhalle** ("Field Marshals' Hall") is a monumental [loggia](/wiki/Loggia "Loggia") on the [Odeonsplatz](/wiki/Odeonsplatz "Odeonsplatz") in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). Modelled after the [Loggia dei Lanzi](/wiki/Loggia_dei_Lanzi "Loggia dei Lanzi") in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), it was commissioned in 1841 by King [Ludwig I of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria") to honour the tradition of the [Bavarian Army](/wiki/Bavarian_Army "Bavarian Army").

In 1923, it was the site of the brief battle that ended Hitler's [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"). During the Nazi era, it served as a monument commemorating the deaths of the 15 Nazis and one bystander killed during the revolt.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feldherrnhalle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Lions_at_the_Feldherrnhalle_in_Munich.JPG/250px-Lions_at_the_Feldherrnhalle_in_Munich.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lions_at_the_Feldherrnhalle_in_Munich.JPG)

Lions at the Feldherrnhalle by [Wilhelm von Rümann](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_R%C3%BCmann "Wilhelm von Rümann")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Feldherrenhalle_2.jpg/250px-Feldherrenhalle_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Feldherrenhalle_2.jpg)

Statue commemorating the Franco-Prussian war inside the Feldherrnhalle

The Feldherrnhalle was built between 1841 and 1844 at the southern end of Munich's [Ludwigstrasse](/wiki/Ludwigstra%C3%9Fe_(Munich) "Ludwigstraße (Munich)") next to the [Palais Preysing](/wiki/Palais_Preysing "Palais Preysing") and southwest of the [Hofgarten](/wiki/Hofgarten_(M%C3%BCnchen) "Hofgarten (München)"). Previously, the [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") *Schwabinger Tor* (gate) occupied that place. [Friedrich von Gärtner](/wiki/Friedrich_von_G%C3%A4rtner "Friedrich von Gärtner") built the Feldherrnhalle[[1]](#cite_note-1) at the behest of King [Ludwig I of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria") after the example of the [Loggia dei Lanzi](/wiki/Loggia_dei_Lanzi "Loggia dei Lanzi") in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence").

The Feldherrnhalle was a symbol of the honours of the Bavarian Army, represented by statues of two military leaders [Johann Tilly](/wiki/Johan_t%27Serclaes,_Count_of_Tilly "Johan t'Serclaes, Count of Tilly") and [Karl Philipp von Wrede](/wiki/Karl_Philipp_von_Wrede "Karl Philipp von Wrede"). The first led Bavarians in the [Thirty Years War](/wiki/Thirty_Years_War "Thirty Years War"); the second led the fight against Napoleon.[[2]](#cite_note-gave-2) The statues were created by [Ludwig Schwanthaler](/wiki/Ludwig_Schwanthaler "Ludwig Schwanthaler").[[3]](#cite_note-guid-3)

Right from the start, some Munich folk used to (and still do) ridicule the two persons honoured in the "Bayerische Feldherrnhalle" (lit. 'Bavarian Hall of Field Commanders / Field Marshals') in reference to the descendance of Tilly and the military strategic capabilities of Wrede:
"The one / first was" indeed "never anything like a Bavarian and the second / other" imputedly "never anything like a Feldherr".
It is a citation from [Lion Feuchtwanger](/wiki/Lion_Feuchtwanger "Lion Feuchtwanger")'s novel *[Erfolg](/w/index.php?title=Success_(Feuchtwanger_novel)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Success (Feuchtwanger novel) (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erfolg_(Roman) "de:Erfolg (Roman)")]*.

A sculptural group by [Ferdinand von Miller](/wiki/Ferdinand_von_Miller "Ferdinand von Miller") was added to the centre of the monument in 1892, after the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War"), representing the victory over the French and the unification of Germany. The lions are a work of [Wilhelm von Rümann](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_R%C3%BCmann "Wilhelm von Rümann"), added in 1906 in imitation of the [Medici lions](/wiki/Medici_lions "Medici lions") of the Loggia dei Lanzi.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Site of the Beer Hall Putsch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feldherrnhalle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Site of the Beer Hall Putsch")]

The Feldherrnhalle was the scene of a confrontation on Friday morning, 9 November 1923, between the [Bavarian State Police](/wiki/Bavarian_State_Police "Bavarian State Police") and the followers of [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Hitler "Hitler") in which the Nazi Party attempted to storm the Bavarian Defense Ministry. This was the culmination of the Nazis' failed coup attempt to take over the Bavarian State, commonly referred to as the *[Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the ensuing gun battle, four policemen and 15 putschists were killed. Many more were wounded, including [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring"). This incident was the beginning of Göring’s addiction to opiates. As a result of the failure of the so-called [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"), Hitler was arrested and sentenced to a prison term.

## Nazi memorial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feldherrnhalle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Nazi memorial")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Feldherrnhalle%2C_Mahnmal%2C_1933.jpg/250px-Feldherrnhalle%2C_Mahnmal%2C_1933.jpg)](/wiki/File:Feldherrnhalle,_Mahnmal,_1933.jpg)

*Mahnmal der Bewegung* as in 1933, installed at the eastern end of the Feldherrnhalle

After the Nazis took power in 1933, Hitler turned the Feldherrnhalle into a memorial to the Nazis killed during the failed putsch. A memorial to the fallen SA men was put up on its east side, opposite the location of the shootings. This monument, called the *Mahnmal der Bewegung*[[5]](#cite_note-5) was created to a design by [Paul Ludwig Troost](/wiki/Paul_Ludwig_Troost "Paul Ludwig Troost"). It was a rectangular structure listing the names of the martyrs.[[2]](#cite_note-gave-2) This was under perpetual ceremonial guard by the SS. The square in front of the Feldherrnhalle (the [Odeonsplatz](/wiki/Odeonsplatz "Odeonsplatz")) was used for SS parades and commemorative rallies. During some of these events the sixteen dead were each commemorated by a temporary pillar placed in the Feldherrnhalle topped by a flame. New SS recruits took their oath of loyalty to Hitler in front of the memorial. Passers-by were expected to hail the site with the [Nazi salute](/wiki/Nazi_salute "Nazi salute").

Consequently, some people tried to avoid this. The structure's back side was (and still is) occupied by a [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") palace, the [Palais Preysing](/wiki/Palais_Preysing "Palais Preysing"), in front of which runs a lane, the [Viscardigasse](/wiki/Viscardigasse "Viscardigasse"). This little detour helped to bypass the hall, subsequently earning it the nickname "Drückebergergasse" (lit. 'shirker's lane').[[2]](#cite_note-gave-2)

One of the most prestigious decorations of the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"), the [Blood Order](/wiki/Blood_Order "Blood Order") medal, featured a relief of the entrance to the Feldherrnhalle on its reverse under an angled [swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika") and sun rays.

## Post-war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feldherrnhalle&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Post-war")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-E12359%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Adolf_Hitler_vor_Feldherrenhalle.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-E12359%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Adolf_Hitler_vor_Feldherrenhalle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-E12359,_M%C3%BCnchen,_Adolf_Hitler_vor_Feldherrenhalle.jpg)

"The solemn act of state in front of the Feldherrnhalle in Munich (11 November 1939) for the seven victims of the criminal bomb attack in Bürgerbräukeller on 8 November 1939" (original caption)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Special_Film_Project_186_-_Feldherrnhalle_2.png/250px-Special_Film_Project_186_-_Feldherrnhalle_2.png)](/wiki/File:Special_Film_Project_186_-_Feldherrnhalle_2.png)

Feldherrnhalle in Munich after American liberation in 1945. American color photography showing inscription being a commentary about [Nazi](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") crimes during the Second World War. Inscription in German language: Concentration camps [Dachau](/wiki/Dachau "Dachau") – [Velden](/wiki/Velden_(Vils) "Velden (Vils)")[[a]](#cite_note-7) – [Buchenwald](/wiki/Buchenwald "Buchenwald"), I am ashamed that I am a German.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

At the end of the war the Feldherrnhalle was restored to its pre-Nazi appearance. Local people spontaneously smashed the *Mahnmal der Bewegung* to pieces on 3 June 1945.[[2]](#cite_note-gave-2) In the 1950s a plan to move Bavaria's memorial to the unknown soldier to the Feldherrnhalle was halted on the grounds that it could provide an excuse for neo-Nazis to meet at the site.[[2]](#cite_note-gave-2)

On 25 April 1995, Reinhold Elstner, a World War II veteran, committed [self-immolation](/wiki/Self-immolation "Self-immolation") in front of Feldhernhalle to protest against "the ongoing official slander and demonization of the German people and German soldiers". Each year neo-fascist groups from various European countries try to hold a commemorative ceremony for him, which [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") authorities try to prevent through state and federal courts.[[8]](#cite_note-p6order-9)
